topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes were thick enough to choke on, mingling with a sour, metallic tang that settled deep in my chest. I stared at the canvas, the vibrant swirl of crimson and obsidian mocking me from the easel. Then I saw it. A faint, almost hidden line tracing the edge of the central figure’s shadow—a precise, intricate design that wasn't mine. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck bunching as I slowly turned, my gaze locking onto the figure standing near the doorway. Their expression was placid, almost serene, which only made the heat rising in my face feel like a physical burn. "What in the blazes is that?" I snapped, my voice sharp enough to cut glass.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking as I ran a thumb over the surface of the oil paint. It felt wrong, tainted. I’d poured weeks of sleepless nights into this piece, a raw expression of my frustration, and now there was this blatant appropriation. When I finally looked up, my eyes narrowed until they were slits. The person standing there seemed utterly unbothered, sipping calmly from a glass of water. A slow, dangerous calm began to creep over my features. "You," I managed, the sound low and vibrating with suppressed fury. "Did you touch this?" I took a deliberate step forward, the floorboards creaking under my weight, every movement calculated to convey the sheer depth of my displeasure.

[story 3]

A cold knot formed beneath my ribs, tightening with every second I observed the alteration. It wasn't just a smudge; it was deliberate, a signature etched into my very creation. I dropped my brush; it clattered loudly against the wooden floor, the sound echoing the sudden, violent surge inside me. I didn't bother with pleasantries. "Stop," I commanded, my tone dangerously flat. I crossed the room in three strides, my breathing shallow and quick. When I reached the individual, I didn't shout. Instead, I leaned in close, my shadow engulfing them, my eyes burning with a frustrated intensity that I hoped was enough to make them understand the gravity of their trespass.
